title: CO₂ emissions per capita vs. fossil fuel consumption per capita
subtitle: Fossil fuel consumption is measured as the average consumption of energy from coal, oil and gas per person. [Fossil fuel and industry emissions](#dod:fossilemissions) are included. [Land-use change emissions](#dod:land-use-change-emissions) are not included.
caption:
CO₂ emissions per capita vs. fossil fuel consumption per capita

Fossil fuel consumption is measured as the average consumption of energy from coal, oil and gas per person. Fossil fuel and industry emissions are included. Land-use change emissions are not included.

The scatter plot displays the relationship between per capita energy from fossil fuels on the horizontal axis and per capita CO₂ emissions on the vertical axis, with both axes plotted on a logarithmic scale. Data points are color-coded by income group: lower-middle-income countries appear in pink, upper-middle-income countries in light green, and high-income countries in dark green. Circle size corresponds to population, with India and China represented by the largest bubbles. Lower-middle-income nations, such as Pakistan and the Philippines, cluster in the bottom-left corner, showing values below 5,000 kWh for energy consumption and under 2 tonnes for emissions. Upper-middle-income countries like Indonesia, Mexico, and Turkey occupy the middle range, generally falling between 10,000 and 20,000 kWh and 2 to 5 tonnes of emissions. High-income countries dominate the upper-right section of the chart. The United States, South Korea, and the Netherlands group between roughly 50,000 and 70,000 kWh with emissions ranging from 10 to 15 tonnes. Qatar sits at the extreme top-right, exceeding 100,000 kWh and roughly 20 tonnes of emissions. Other labeled high-income entities include Japan, the United Kingdom, Ireland, Singapore, Saudi Arabia, and Kuwait, which span a wide gradient from approximately 20,000 kWh to over 100,000 kWh. The visual distribution indicates a positive correlation where higher fossil fuel consumption per person aligns with higher CO₂ emissions per person across the labeled nations.
